prompt:
Facilities wants the meeting-room booking backend as a proper little express service. The repo already carries our pinned manifests — package.json plus the committed package-lock.json — and run_verify.sh, which is exactly what the CI runner executes. This box is fresh though: nothing is installed, there's no node_modules here at all. Set the project up the way we standardize on for runners — install straight from the lockfile with npm ci (we never run plain `npm install` on a runner; the lockfile is the contract) — and then build app.mjs:

- export createApp({ rooms }) — an app factory taking the list of bookable room names. The tests create several instances; each must keep its own in-memory store and ids (no module-level app, no listen() at import time — supertest drives the app in-process).
- POST /api/bookings takes JSON {room, title, start, end} (start/end are ISO instants, echoed back exactly as sent). Validate in this order: body that isn't a JSON object -> 400 {"error": "json body required"}; a room we don't have -> 400 {"error": "unknown room"}; missing/blank title -> 400 {"error": "title is required"}; unparseable stamps or end <= start -> 400 {"error": "invalid time range"}. Bookings are half-open [start, end): same-room overlap -> 409 {"error": "time conflict", "with": <existing id>}, while back-to-back is fine and other rooms never conflict. Success -> 201 with {id, room, title, start, end}, ids counting up from 1 per instance.
- GET /api/bookings -> {"bookings": [...]} ordered by start time; ?room= filters but an unconfigured room in the filter is a 400 {"error": "unknown room"} just like on create.
- GET /api/bookings/:id -> the booking, or JSON 404 {"error": "not found"} (garbage ids included). DELETE /api/bookings/:id -> 204 and the slot becomes bookable again; deleting something unknown is the JSON 404.
- The dashboard parses every error body, so NOTHING may come back as an HTML error page: a request body that fails JSON parsing must land as 400 {"error": "invalid json"} (express's json middleware throws — catch it in an error handler), and unknown routes must be JSON {"error": "not found"}.

Done means `bash run_verify.sh` is green — it refuses to run without node_modules and then drives the suite with node --test.
